preamble: The shift from the alveolar stop [d] to the alveolar nasal [n] is a classic example of nasal assimilation. When a voiced stop occurs in a cluster with a nasal consonant (like [nd]), it frequently assimilates the nasal manner of articulation, resulting in a geminate [nn].

examples.note: Regressive nasal assimilation: the [d] in [nd] cluster assimilates to the preceding nasal, yielding geminate [nn].
Meinhof's Law: voiced stop in prenasalized cluster assimilates to nasal when the following syllable also contains a prenasalized consonant.

In Trans-New Guinea languages including Kalam, the Proto-TNG prenasalized stop *ⁿd undergoes simplification to a plain alveolar nasal [n] in certain phonological environments, particularly word-finally. Kalam orthographic /d/ represents an historically prenasalized stop that has retained or partially lost its oral closure component across TNG subgroups.

Huli (Engan branch, Southern Highlands, Papua New Guinea) exhibits reduction of word-final prenasalized stops to their nasal components. The Proto-Trans-New Guinea *ⁿd series is realized as [n] in syllable-final position, consistent with the broader Trans-New Guinea tendency to restrict final consonant inventories to nasals and sonorants.

sources: Campbell, L. (2013). Historical Linguistics: An Introduction. MIT Press.
